coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly Coccinia Grandis-derived excitation-dependent fluorescence of carbon dots for In-Vivo bioimaging and fluorescent ink applications
coredata.dc:creator: Karuppasamy, Bavya Devi
Perumal, Suguna
Atchudan, Raji
Sundramoorthy, Ashok K.
Manoj, Devaraj
Sambasivam, Sangaraju
Karthik, Namachivayam
Kamaraj, Eswaran
Suresh Kumar, Raju
Ramalingam, Srinivasan
Rok Lee, Yong
coredata.dc:description: This study adopted an eco-friendly approach for the one-step hydrothermal conversion of sustainable Coccinia grandis into well-dispersed, uniform-sized carbon dots. The resulting eco-friendly carbon dots (EC-Dots) were harnessed for bioimaging and fluorescent ink applications. EC-Dots exhibited an average size of 4 nm. Further, cytotoxicity assessments on live nematodes revealed cell viability > 95 %. In-vivo studies showed excellent uptake and their ability to emit light in multiple colors, including blue, green, and red, achieved by adjusting the excitation wavelength. Additionally, the EC-Dots exhibited robust fluorescence in aqueous solutions and exceptional resistance to photobleaching, rendering them highly effective for generating fluorescent ink for drawing and writing. Thus, this research showcases an environmentally sustainable, cost-effective, and straightforward hydrothermal method for creating biocompatible EC-Dots with diverse hydrophilic properties. These synthesized EC-Dots stand as a promising platform for applications in fluorescent ink and in vivo bioimaging, offering a high level of safety with minimal cytotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coccinia grandis
Hydrothermal
Carbon dot
Caenorhabditis elegans
Bioimaging
Fluorescent ink

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The project was supported by the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R142), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of one-step hydrothermal processing of ecologically sustainable Coccinia Grandis into well-dispersed carbon dots synthesis.
(a) X-ray diffraction pattern of synthesized EC-Dots and (b) Raman spectrum of synthesized EC-Dots.
(a–e) FESEMimages of synthesized EC-Dots. Electron microscope EDX mapping images of EC-Dots (f) carbon, (g) oxygen, and (h) nitrogen elements; (i) Overlapping EDX mapping image.
(a–c) TEMimages of synthesized EC-Dots and (d) Particle size distribution chart of synthesized EC-Dots.
(a) FTIR spectrum of synthesized EC-Dots; (b) XPS C 1s region of synthesized EC-Dots; (c) XPS N 1s region of synthesized EC-Dots; (d) XPS O 1s region of synthesized EC-Dots.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum (Inset: photographs of EC-Dots aqueous solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right)), (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra and (c) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized EC-Dots.

(a) Prolong stability for synthesized EC-Dots (inset: digital photographic images EC-Dots aqueous solution under UV light at the time of synthesis (left) after 150 days (right)); (b) Photostability under UV exposure for synthesized EC-Dots (inset: digital photographic images EC-Dots aqueous solution under UV light by varying the irradiation times 0 min (left) and after 100 min (right)).

Confocal microscopeimages. (a–c) Caenorhabditis elegans cell culture incubated with synthesized EC-Dots excited in 400 nm (Blue), 470 nm (Green), and 550 nm (Red); (d) Caenorhabditis elegans cell culture at the bright field. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Photograph of fluorescent “Coccinia Grandis/Carbon dots” patterns and words written by using the aqueous carbon dots solution under 365 nm UV lamp.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly Coccinia Grandis-derived excitation-dependent fluorescence of carbon dots for In-Vivo bioimaging and fluorescent ink applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study adopted an eco-friendly approach for the one-step hydrothermal conversion of sustainable Coccinia grandis into well-dispersed, uniform-sized carbon dots. The resulting eco-friendly carbon dots (EC-Dots) were harnessed for bioimaging and fluorescent ink applications. EC-Dots exhibited an average size of 4 nm. Further, cytotoxicity assessments on live nematodes revealed cell viability > 95 %. In-vivo studies showed excellent uptake and their ability to emit light in multiple colors, including blue, green, and red, achieved by adjusting the excitation wavelength. Additionally, the EC-Dots exhibited robust fluorescence in aqueous solutions and exceptional resistance to photobleaching, rendering them highly effective for generating fluorescent ink for drawing and writing. Thus, this research showcases an environmentally sustainable, cost-effective, and straightforward hydrothermal method for creating biocompatible EC-Dots with diverse hydrophilic properties. These synthesized EC-Dots stand as a promising platform for applications in fluorescent ink and in vivo bioimaging, offering a high level of safety with minimal cytotoxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of eco-friendly and biocompatible materials for a wide range of applications has gained significant attention in both industry and scientific research [1–4]. Carbon dots (C-Dots) have emerged as an interesting topic of research due to their remarkable optical properties, small size, biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness [5–7]. The scientific community and industry have shown a great deal of interest because of their versatility and potential applications, including in vivo bioimaging and environmentally friendly fluorescent ink [8–11]. Utilizing sustainable and economical sources and aligning with the growing interest in green and eco-friendly methods, C-Dots are synthesized using various plant sources. These plant-based sources possess excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and are rich in carbon content making them an advantageous source for the synthesis of green C-Dots [12–14]. In general, common heteroatoms like nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B), and phosphorous (P) are incorporated into carbon dots [15,16]. The addition of these heteroatoms can significantly enhance the electronic structure and surface properties of carbon dots, leading to improved or tailored performance for various applications [17–19]. This versatility makes doped carbon dots valuable materials for applications ranging from bioimaging and sensing to catalysis and optoelectronics [20].

During the last decade, various routes have been proposed to synthesize C-Dots including the hydrothermal method [21], microwave-assisted synthesis [22], pyrolysis [23], electrochemical synthesis [24], chemical oxidation [25], solvothermal method [26], ultrasonic irradiation [27], and laser ablation [28] based on the desired properties, size, and surface functionalization of the C-Dots. The hydrothermal method has various advantages over other methods, including being green and environmentally friendly, controlling the particle size and properties, high yield and purity, low energy consumption, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and tunable properties [29]. In addition, the choice of the precursor and reaction conditions also play an important role in tailoring the properties of the C-Dots [30]. Researchers select the most appropriate synthesis method based on factors such as control over properties, environmental considerations, and scalability. Recently, the studies have focused on the eco-friendly synthesis of C-Dots. Researchers have utilized plant, animal, and food waste to fabricate C-Dots for bioimaging and fluorescent ink applications [9,12,31–41]. By repurposing these waste materials, researchers are able to create biocompatible C-Dots that can be used for fluorescence-based imaging of biological structures and processes. Their small size and excellent biocompatibility make them ideal candidates for use in various bioimaging techniques, providing valuable insights into the world of biology and medicine [42–44].

In the present study, we have used Coccinia Grandis (CG), also known as Ivy Goaurd or Tindora, an eco-friendly source for the synthesis of C-Dots, aligning with sustainability and green chemistry principles. CG-derived C-Dots have the potential to be used in various bioimaging techniques, such as fluorescence microscopy, confocal imaging, and in-vivo imaging. Further, the bright fluorescence of CG-derived C-Dots in an aqueous solution stands for them as an effective component in the development of eco-friendly fluorescent ink. Such ink can find its applications in art, signage, document security, and other practical uses that prioritize sustainability.

Herein, CG was hydrothermally carbonized for the production of C-Dots. A typical synthesis is as follows: Initially, the received CG was dried under sunlight, and then the dried CG was pulverized well by a commercial mixer and grinder. Afterward, 100 mg of crushed CG was dispersed in 50 mL of double distilled water. The CG powder (CGP)-dispersed mixture was transferred to a 100 mL capacity of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and 25 % of ammonia solution (NH4OH) was added to adjust the pH 7.5–8.0 and then hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 24 h in a hot-air oven. After the chosen period, the autoclave was allowed to cool until it reached room temperature for a final reacted solution mixture. The final product of the expected C-Dots was obtained from the reaction mixture via a simple filtration using a microporous mixed cellulose ester membrane filter with pore sizes of 0.22 µm. The obtained supernatant (C-Dots) solution was collected and dried in a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain C-Dots in a powder form and denoted eco-friendly C-Dots (EC-Dots). The details of the synthesis of EC-Dots are displayed in Fig. 1 . The structural and optical properties of the obtained EC-Dots were examined using various physicochemical analytical techniques. Finally, the EC-Dots were applied for potential applications based on their structural and optical properties.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern showed a broad of synthesized EC-Dots derived from CG is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The XRD pattern revealed the presence of a dominant peak at 23°, which is (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials, and a small peak at 42.5° linked to disordered carbon atoms and associated with the (100) plane. The fact that the peaks are broad implies that the carbon structure is not highly crystalline. It has a degree of disorder or stacking variations in the carbon layers. The formed pattern confirms the formation of an amorphous or disordered structure, which means the atoms are not arranged in a regular, repeating crystal lattice [45]. As a result, no sharp diffraction pattern was seen. The most prominent peak occurs at around 23°, and the shift in the peak positions or changes in peak broadness can provide insights into the degree of graphitization or ordering within the EC-Dots. The d-spacing of 0.39 nm for the (002) peak corresponds to the spacing between adjacent (002) planes in the carbon structure. This spacing is larger compared to highly crystalline graphite, indiacating that there is some degree of disorder or expansion in the carbon layers. The d-spacing of 0.21 nm for the (100) peak represents the spacing between adjacent (100) planes in the hexagonal lattice. This d-spacing value is indicative of the expanded or disordered carbon structure compared to ideal graphite. Thus, the larger d-spacing values suggest that the carbon layers in the EC-CDs are not as tightly packed as in crystalline graphite, confirming the partially ordered or disordered nature of the carbon structure in the C-Dots [46–48]. The Raman spectroscopy technique was also used to determine the graphitization degree of the carbon structure. The exhibited D and G bands in the Raman spectra are shown in Fig. 2(b). The D band is typically found around 1355 cm−1 and is associated with defects and disorder in the carbon structure [49]. The G band is usually observed around 1585 cm−1, representing the sp2 hybridized carbon–carbon bonds in the EC-Dots [50]. The ratio of D to G band intensity, at 0.75, indicates a partially disordered graphite-like structure of the EC-Dots with a reasonable degree of graphitization. These results are in accordance with the XRD.

Fig. 3 (a–e) illustrates the SEM image of EC-Dots prepared via the hydrothermal method (200 °C for 24 h). SEM images show the uniform distribution of tiny wrinkle-shaped surfaces, suggesting that the C-Dots are relatively well-dispersed and homogeneous in size distribution. The unique morphology of the wrinkle-shaped surface may influence its optical properties and surface chemistry interactions with other materials. Fig. S1 indicates the presence of carbon along with lesser amounts of other constituents. The different elements are highlighted in multiple colors in elemental mapping over the same area, as the quantitative assessment was not feasible [51]. EX elemental mapping Fig. 3 (f–i) showed that the product was made up of three elements namely carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the levels of 80 %, 16 %, and 4 %, respectively. This result confirms that carbon is the major element in the synthesized EC-Dots. The oxygen and nitrogen can be related to the functional groups on the EC-Dots such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups [52]. Further, inorder to analyze the particle morphology, a versatile and powerful tool TEM was used for imaging and characterizing nanoscale particles less than 10 nm.

The high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 4 (a–c)) of the synthesized EC-Dots show the amorphous nature of the material. For the investigation, the synthesized EC-Dots were drop-coated onto a carbon-coated copper grid. The presence of uniform morphology and even distribution indicates the quality and consistency of the synthesized EC-CDs. In addition, the even distribution implies that the synthesized EC-CDs are uniformly dispersed throughout the sample and this even distribution is often a desirable feature for various applications including sensors, imaging, and drug delivery. Fig. 4c shows that the developed EC-Dots appeared as bright spots against a darker background. This brightness is due to the high electron density of the carbon atoms in the dots. The inset Fig. 4c provides the interlayer distance of 0.21 nm and its partial amorphous nature. The observed d-spacing of 0.21 nm for the (100) peak in the TEM analysis represents the distance between adjacent (100) planes in the hexagonal lattice of the synthesized EC-dots. This value is an ideal graphene d-spacing of 0.21 nm (100) planes and matches well with calculated values of XRD results [53]. Fig. 4d shows the size distribution of the corresponding TEM image ( Fig. 4b), the size distribution graphs clearly showed a narrow size distribution of EC-dots in the range of 2–6 nm with an average size of 4 nm [54].

Furthermore, attenuated total reflection (ATR)-Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to analyze the specific spectral features of the sample providing detailed insights into their chemical structure. ( Fig. 5 (a)) shows the FT-IR spectra of synthesized EC-Dots. The presence of broad and intense peaks at 3350 cm−1 and 3260 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH groups, respectively [55]. The CH asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations are responsible for the absorption bands found at 2926 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1, respectively [56]. The CH symmetric stretching mode is normally observed at slightly lower wavenumbers compared to asymmetric mode typically in the 2900–2800 cm−1 range. The presence of carbonyl groups (CO) groups, resulted in characteristic peaks around 1670 cm−1. In addition, the presence of CC, CN, COH, COC, and –CH2 bonds in the carbon framework is formed at 1580, 1405, 1300, 1140–1020, and 650 cm−1, respectively [57]. The intensity and the position of the peaks in the spectrum provided detailed information about the surface passivation or functionalization of the C-Dots thereby confirming the presence of –NH2, –COOH, and –OH groups on the surface of EC-Dots. Thus, both hydroxyl and amino groups can make the synthesized EC-Dots hydrophilic, allowing them to disperse well in aqueous solutions. Additionally, these groups can influence the optical properties, dispersibility/solubility, and surface chemistry of EC-CDs.

XPS was employed to analyze the elemental states, surface functionalization, and chemical compositions of the EC-Dots prepared from CG. The three prominent peaks in the EC-Dots survey spectrum of XPS (Fig. S2), which correspond to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, are seen at 285, 400, and 532 eV, respectively. The C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s binding energy levels were further deconvoluted and fitted using Casa XPS software in order to identify the chemical bond information. After deconvolution, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s ( Fig. 5(b)) reveal five component peaks with binding energies of approximately 284.58, 285.28, 286.28, 287.58, and 288.68 eV. The peak centered at 284.58 eV is attributed to CC and CC bonding. Further, the bonding state at this energy reflects the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms typically found in aromatic or unsaturated carbon structures. The CC bonds typically appear at lower binding energies than CC bonds. The peak at 285.28 eV corresponds to the CNC/CNH groups. The peak at 286.28 eV corresponds to CH and potentially COH/COC groups associated with organic compounds [56]. Further, the peaks at 287.58 and 288.68 eV correspond to CO/CN and OCOH groups, respectively [58]. The deconvolution of N 1s XPS peak ( Fig. 5(c)) provides a detailed breakdown of the nitrogen species and their chemical states in the synthesized materials. Further, the deconvolution suggests the presence of different nitrogen species and their respective chemical environments. The peak at 397.98 eV corresponds to nitrogen atoms involved in CNC bonds [59]. This binding energy suggests the presence of nitrogen atoms that are part of carbon–nitrogen-carbon functional groups. The peak at 399.58 eV indicated the presence of NH bonds related to amine functionalities. Later, the component at 400.58 eV is associated with (C)3–N bonding, representing a unique type of nitrogen-carbon bonding on the surface of the prepared material. The deconvolution of O 1s XPS spectrum ( Fig. 5(d)) showed two major component peaks with the specified binding energies of about 531.38 and 532.68 eV for the COH/COC and CO/COOH/HOH functional groups respectively, confirming the existence of oxygen groups on the material’s surface [60,61]. Thus, in agreement with the FTIR data, the XPS spectra confirmed the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH on the surfaces of the prepared EC-Dots [62].

The aqueous solution was used to record the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra in order to examine the optical characteristics of the synthesized EC-Dots ( Fig. 6
). The UV–vis absorbance spectra of the synthesized EC-Dots ( Fig. 6a) typically show the various absorption features corresponding to different electronic transitions. The sharp peaks around 220 and 270 nm are associated with π–π* electronic transitions (CC). Further, the wide shoulder area between 300 and 330 nm, which might be attributed to the n–π* transition (CO) of the nanocarbon [31,63,64]. The emission spectra and fluorescence excitation of the highly water-dispersible synthesized EC-Dots are shown in Fig. 6b. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission levels were seen at 350 and 416 nm, respectively, as seen in the spectra. This result suggests that the maximum fluorescence emission resulted at 416 nm following excitation at 350 nm.

Further, the fluorescence emissions were measured at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 420 nm with the aim to investigate the excitation-dependent emission features. The fluorescence intensity scaled progressively to an optimal level and subsequently decreased as the excitation wavelength increased. With a stokes shift of 66 nm, the excitation wavelength of 350 nm produced the maximum fluorescence. Furthermore, a shift in peak to longer wavelengths and an increase in excitement are seen; these observations are evident from the normalized spectra of excitation-dependent emission (Fig. S3). These findings clearly imply that the fluorescence emissions of the prepared EC-Dots were excitation-dependent [65].

The detailed mechanism of carbon dots emitting light of different colors under different excitation wavelengths involves a combination of quantum effects, size-dependent properties, surface states, and chemical composition [66]. Quantum confinement in smaller dots results in a larger bandgap, leading to higher energy emissions in the blue or green spectrum. Larger dots with a smaller bandgap emit lower energy light in the yellow, orange, or red spectrum [67]. Surface passivation with functional groups introduces energy levels, influencing the emission spectrum [17]. Solvent and environmental conditions impact emission properties by altering surface states and energy levels. Quantum confinement occurs when dot size approaches the exciton Bohr radius, leading to discrete electronic states and varied bandgap energies. The π- π* transition in aromatic structures contributes to light absorption and emission, influencing colors under different excitation wavelengths [68]. In essence, carbon dot emission results from the interplay of quantum effects, surface chemistry, size-dependent bandgap tuning, and structural factors.

Further, the quantum yield (QY) measurements are valuable for characterizing the photoluminescent properties of C-Dots and determining their efficiency in converting absorbed energy into emitted light. The QY of the synthesized EC-Dots is calculated to be 17 % using quinine sulphate as a reference. Further, the quantum yield and optical properties of the synthesized carbon dots are compared with the different natural sources which is shown in Table S1. The obtained results are comparable with the existing ones.

In particular, for real-time optical applications, the critical parameters are the long-term stability and photostability of the synthesized EC-Dots. The fluorescence intensity of EC-Dots aqueous solution was examined at various storage intervals (1–150 days) after it was kept in storage for a lengthy period of time ( Fig. 7 (a)). Even after being stored for several months, there was no noticeable aggregation or shift in fluorescence intensity, which may be due to the surface functionalization of EC-Dots, which can introduce the charges or polar functional groups, which can further enhance electrostatic repulsions and maintain stability between the EC-Dots [38,69,70]. The inset Fig. 7(a) exhibits the fluorescence emission of EC-Dots aqueous solution under the 365 nm UV lamp at different storage times. This suggests that the EC-Dots have long-lasting, steady fluorescence.

The EC-Dots aqueous solution was exposed to UV light (8 W) at 365 nm excitation for 100 min in order to determine the photobleaching stability. Further, fluorescence intensity was then monitored at 25-minute intervals of time. The variations in the fluorescence intensity of EC-Dots at various irradiation durations (0–100 min) are shown in Fig. 7(b). Even after 100 min under intense UV light, EC-Dots still exhibit their initial fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, it is evident from the inset (Fig. 7(b)) that the stability of the synthesized EC-Dots under UV irradiation showed minimal or insignificant changes in fluorescence following irradiation [32].

The superior aqueous stability and increased adjustable fluorescence of the easily synthesized EC-Dots from CG motivated us to further explore the fluorescent and bioimaging applications. The cytotoxicity of the prepared material was assessed in live-cell lines (nematodes/Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans)) for the bioimaging applications. Even at larger doses (Fig. S4), there is no appreciable reduction in nematode viability thereby confirming the superior biocompatibility of the fabricated EC-Dots. Up to the concentration of 250 µg mL−1, the toxicity assay indicated almost negligible toxicity, and the cell viability is higher than 95 % [8]. The developed material showed a promising choice for bioimaging and other biomedical uses with its strong fluorescence, excellent photostability, and high water dispersibility.

Further, using a fluorescent microscope, in vivo cellular imaging and toxicity experiments were carried out on the C. elegans/nematodes and incubated with EC-Dots produced from CG. The C. elegans cultured with EC-Dots showed blue, green, and red fluorescence emissions by adjusting the excitation wavelength filters at 400, 470, and 550 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8 . Every portion of the nematodes (C. elegans) appeared brilliantly multicolored indicating that the EC-Dots can readily permeate the nematode’s whole body and this is a key feature for bioimaging, as it allows the comprehensive and detailed imaging of the entire organism [71]. In addition, the hydrophilic nature of EC-Dots is advantageous because it helps to prevent aggregation and facilitates dispersion in the aqueous environment of the nematode [3]. It also promotes their permeation throughout the organism. Research in the areas of developmental biology, neuroscience, and other fields can benefit from the application of synthesized EC-Dots.

Digital photographs of the hand-drawn images are shown in Fig. 9 after being energized by a portable UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. The hand-drawn images are clearly visible and produce intense blue fluorescence. Further, the hand-drawn image was kept for three months and compared to ascertain the fluorescence stability. The fluorescence intensity showed no discernible changes even after three months of storage under static conditions, indicating that the synthesized EC-Dots possess good fluorescence stability. Thus, the synthesized EC-Dots are particularly attractive for fluorescent ink applications due to their low toxicity, excellent fluorescence properties, and ease of functionalization [72]. These unique and tunable fluorescence properties attract a versatile choice for creating eye-catching and functional prints for various industries and anti-counterfeiting purposes.

The multifunctional EC-Dots were synthesized from CG, one of the easily and enormously available vegetables via a cost-effective one-step hydrothermal method. The XRD peaks confirmed the amorphous carbon structure. The surface morphology showed a uniform globular shape with an average particle size of 4 nm. Further, FT-IR and XPS confirmed the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups enhancing higher water-dispersable properties. In addition, EC-Dots showed redshifted excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior with an acceptable QY of 17 %. The synthesized EC-Dots exhibited excellent prolonged stability, good biocompatibility, and negligible cytotoxicity even at elevated concentrations (250 µg mL−1). Together, the current research provided a new platform in the fields of bioimaging and a versatile component in various types of fluorescent ink due to their exceptional fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization.

Bavya Devi Karuppasamy: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Suguna Perumal: Writing – review & editing. Raji Atchudan: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ashok K. Sundramoorthy: Visualization, Investigation. Devaraj Manoj: Investigation, Formal analysis. Sangaraju Sambasivam: Investigation, Formal analysis. Namachivayam Karthik: Visualization, Formal analysis. Eswaran Kamaraj: Visualization, Formal analysis. Raju Suresh Kumar: Funding acquisition. Srinivasan Ramalingam: Validation, Visualization. Yong Rok Lee: Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R142), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: Materials, Instrumentation methods, quantum yield measurements, photobleaching and prolonging stability measurements, nematode killing assay, fluorescent staining, and imaging of the obtained EC-Dots. Fig. S1. EDX spectrum of synthesized EC-Dots; Fig. S2. XPS survey spectrum of synthesized EC-Dots; Fig. S3. Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission normalized spectra of synthesized EC-Dots; Fig. S4. Cell viability MTT assay results: The bar chart provides a comparison of the viabilities of EC-Dots treated nematodes and untreated nematodes (control). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2024.117300.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eco-friendly graphitic carbon nitride nanomaterials for the development of innovative biomaterials: preparation, properties, opportunities, current trends, and future outlook
J. Saudi Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Khan, K. Qi, I. Khan, F. Wu, D. Gao, M. Humayun, A. Khan, A. Bahadur, A. Faleh Alanazi, M. Bououdina, Eco-friendly graphitic carbon nitride nanomaterials for the development of innovative biomaterials: preparation, properties, opportunities, current trends, and future outlook, Journal of Saudi Chemical Society (2023) 101753. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jscs.2023.101753.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eco-friendly, bright luminescent carbon dots and their potential applications for detecting hypochlorous acid in water and live cell imaging
J. Mater. Res. Technol.
